questions.question: Define polynomial time and explain why it is considered the boundary between tractable and intractable problems.
keyPoints: Polynomial time: O(n^k) for constant k
Stable boundary - polynomials closed under composition
Reasonable scaling with input growth
Separates practical from impractical runtimes

questions.question: Explain the difference between the classes P and NP. Is P = NP? Justify your answer.
keyPoints: P: Problems solvable in polynomial time
NP: Problems verifiable in polynomial time
P ⊆ NP is proven
P = NP is an unsolved problem (Millennium Prize)

questions.question: What is polynomial-time reduction? How is it used to prove a problem is NP-complete?
keyPoints: Transformation from problem A to problem B in poly-time
If B can be solved easily, A can too
Used to show NP-hardness
For NP-complete: must be in NP + all NP problems reduce to it

questions.question: State and explain the Cook-Levin theorem. Why is SAT important in complexity theory?
keyPoints: Cook-Levin theorem (1971): SAT is NP-complete
First problem proven NP-complete
All problems in NP reduce to SAT
Foundation for proving other problems NP-complete

questions.question: What are the implications if P = NP? Discuss the practical consequences.
keyPoints: All NP-complete problems become polynomial-time solvable
Cryptography would be broken (RSA, etc.)
Optimization problems become tractable
Mathematical proofs could be automated

questions.question: Differentiate between pseudopolynomial time and true polynomial time with an example.
keyPoints: Pseudopoly: polynomial in numeric value, not input length
Example: Knapsack DP O(nW) where W is weight
True poly: O(n^2) depends on input size n
Distinction matters for large numbers

questions.question: What do you understand by NP-complete problems? List some well-known NP-complete problems and explain how you would prove a new problem is NP-complete.
keyPoints: NP-complete: in NP + NP-hard
Examples: SAT, Clique, Vertex Cover, TSP, Hamiltonian Path
Prove: (1) show in NP with polynomial verifier, (2) reduce known NP-complete to it in poly-time
Use transitivity of reduction
If one NP-complete is in P, then P = NP

questions.question: Explain the P vs NP problem in detail. Why is it important? What would happen if someone proves P = NP? Discuss the impact on cryptography, optimization, and algorithm design.
keyPoints: P vs NP asks if verifiable = solvable in poly time
One of 7 Millennium Prize Problems
If P = NP: all NP-complete problems become tractable
RSA, cryptographic systems broken
Optimization problems like TSP become polynomial
If P ≠ NP: NP-complete problems remain intractable
Profound implications for mathematics and computing

questions.question: Design an algorithm to determine if a boolean formula in CNF (Conjunctive Normal Form) is satisfiable. Analyze its time complexity. Is this problem tractable? Justify.
keyPoints: SAT problem - NP-complete
Brute force: try all 2^n assignments = O(2^n) exponential
No known polynomial-time algorithm exists
If P = NP, would have poly-time solution
Currently only exponential/heuristic approaches
CNF-SAT remains intractable in practice for large n

questions.question: Compare and contrast tractable and intractable problems. Discuss how complexity theory helps in classifying problems and making practical algorithmic choices.
keyPoints: Tractable: solvable in polynomial time O(n^k)
Intractable: require super-polynomial (exponential) time
Complexity theory provides formal classification
P, NP, NP-complete, NP-hard classes
Guides algorithm selection and approximation
Even NP-complete problems often have good approximations
